title: The Valley Before the Mountain
bullets: The Valley of Oaxaca is an upside-down Y of fertile highland valley floor about 600 square miles in area, ringed by mountains and watered by summer rains at roughly 5,000 feet elevation.
San José Mogote in the Etla arm became the largest settlement in the valley by 1400 BCE, growing to over 1,000 people with public buildings, magnetite mirror workshops, and Olmec trade connections.

Structure 28 at San José Mogote, a temple mound standing over 15 feet tall with stuccoed walls and a stairway, was one of the earliest monumental constructions in highland Mesoamerica around 1150 BCE.

The Tierras Largas phase from 1500 to 1150 BCE produced small farming hamlets with distinctive grayware pottery fired in oxygen-reduced kilns, a ceramic fingerprint that would identify Zapotec material culture for three thousand years.
Milpa farming combined maize, beans, and squash in the same field, providing a complete diet of carbohydrates, protein, and vitamins that sustained the valley's growing population.

Around 1100 BCE, Olmec-style artifacts including carved greenstone figurines and specific pottery motifs appeared at San José Mogote, evidence of trade and cultural contact between the Gulf Coast and the Oaxacan highlands.

By 900 BCE the valley had split into three rival chiefdoms in the Etla, Tlacolula, and Zimatlán arms, with some settlements moving to defensible hilltop locations and burned buildings appearing at several sites.

Monument 3 at San José Mogote, carved around 600 BCE, shows a dead or sacrificed captive with a calendrical name glyph between its feet, possibly the oldest written text in Mesoamerica and the prototype for the Danzantes.

title: Building Monte Albán
bullets: Around 500 BCE a coalition of valley communities founded Monte Albán on a jagged mountain ridge 1,300 feet above the valley floor, leveling the summit to create an artificial mesa, one of the most ambitious landscape-engineering projects in the pre-Columbian Americas.

Archaeologist Richard Blanton proposed that Monte Albán was a disembedded capital, a new center deliberately placed in neutral territory where the three valley arms converge so no existing community could claim it as their own.

The Main Plaza was roughly 1,000 by 650 feet of packed earth flanked by temple platforms, administrative buildings, and the massive North Platform at the northern end, one of the earliest planned urban spaces in the Western Hemisphere.

Building J, an arrow-shaped structure oriented at a 45-degree angle to everything else on the site, may have been an astronomical observatory aligned to the star Capella and the zenith passage of the sun.

Over 40 conquest slabs on Building J show place-name glyphs with inverted heads of defeated rulers, the earliest known territorial conquest records in Mesoamerica, political propaganda carved in stone.
Building L housed the Danzantes, over 300 carved stone slabs depicting naked, mutilated captives with calendrical name glyphs, the founding statement of Zapotec military power at Monte Albán.

The mountaintop had no natural water source, so the Zapotec relied on cisterns, carried water from the valley floor, and eventually canal systems, making every drink of water an act of infrastructure.
By 200 BCE Monte Albán had absorbed every rival center in all three valley arms, established frontier outposts in the Cuicatlán Cañada to the north, and reached a valley population of roughly 50,000.

title: The Danzantes and the First Writing
bullets: The more than 300 Danzantes carvings on Building L depict naked, contorted figures with closed eyes, open mouths, and evidence of mutilation, now understood as slain or sacrificed captives, not dancers as 19th-century scholars assumed.

The Zapotec script was a logographic writing system combining calendar signs, name glyphs, and place signs that could record who conquered whom, when, and where, used for at least a thousand years from roughly 600 BCE.

The piye was the Zapotec 260-day ritual calendar combining 20 named days with 13 numbers, producing 260 unique combinations used for naming ceremonies, divination, and identifying individuals by their birthday.
The yza was the 365-day solar calendar divided into 18 months of 20 days plus a dangerous 5-day residual, interlocking with the piye to create a 52-year Calendar Round shared across Mesoamerica.

Whether the Olmec Cascajal Block from around 900 BCE or the Zapotec Monument 3 from around 600 BCE represents the earliest Mesoamerican writing is genuinely unresolved, though both predate Maya texts by centuries.

The Zapotec language, called Diidxazá by its speakers, belongs to the Oto-Manguean family, completely unrelated to Mayan, Mixe-Zoquean, or Uto-Aztecan, as different from Olmec speech as English is from Chinese.

The Zapotec called themselves Be'ena' Za'a, meaning Cloud People, believing their ancestors descended from clouds, rocks, trees, and jaguars, a myth reflected in their preference for mountaintop settlements.

The Danzantes show specific individuals identified by calendrical names, making the practice personal: each slab was a named person, their humiliation made permanent in stone, predating similar Maya captive stelae by centuries.

title: Monte Albán at Its Height
bullets: By around 400 CE Monte Albán reached an estimated 25,000 people, with most living on more than 2,000 terraced residential platforms carved into hillsides radiating out from the ceremonial center in every direction.

Teotihuacan in the Valley of Mexico, a metropolis of 100,000 to 200,000 at its peak, maintained a Zapotec residential neighborhood called the Oaxaca Barrio with Oaxacan-style pottery, tombs, and funerary urns.
Monte Albán showed no signs of conquest by Teotihuacan: the Zapotec kept their own writing, religion, and political structure, suggesting a diplomatic relationship of equals rather than domination.
The Mesoamerican ballgame, played with a solid rubber ball using hips, shoulders, and forearms, was practiced at Monte Albán's I-shaped ball court in the northeast corner of the Main Plaza.

Tomb 104 from around 200 CE contained spectacular polychrome murals depicting processional figures and deities in red, yellow, blue, and green, the finest surviving examples of Classic Zapotec painting.
Over a thousand ceramic funerary urns recovered from Monte Albán depict seated deities or ancestors with elaborate headdresses and costumes, placed in tombs as companions and guardians for the dead.

Elite Zapotec families reopened their family tombs repeatedly over generations, depositing new burials alongside old bones, practicing ancestor veneration as a functioning political institution where legitimacy derived from prestigious dead.

Genealogical stelae recording rulers' names, parentage, conquests, and ritual acts show a shift from early propaganda about military conquest to later emphasis on dynastic lineage and inherited legitimacy.
Zapotec engineers constructed canal irrigation systems in the valley to intensify maize agriculture and feed the growing mountaintop population, making the city a parasite on the valley below.

title: The Classic Zapotec World
bullets: Cocijo, the god of lightning, rain, and fertility, was the dominant figure in Zapotec religious art, depicted with a forked serpent tongue and elaborate headdress on urns, reliefs, and tomb walls more than any other deity.

The Zapotec organized their deities under Pitao, a concept meaning great time or infinity, the underlying creative force of the universe manifesting through specific gods governing different domains of life and nature.
The Zapotec underworld, called Lyobaa or place of rest, was not a place of punishment but where the dead continued their existence, with family tombs understood as literal entrances to this realm.

Tomb murals were painted for the dead inside sealed chambers that were covered with earth after final burial, creating a painted environment for the dead to inhabit in flat, linear style similar to Egyptian tomb painting.

Most Monte Albán residents were farmers living in wattle-and-daub houses on terraced hillsides, growing maize on the irrigated valley floor and supplementing their diet with beans, squash, chili peppers, and avocados.

Zapotec rulers pierced their own tongues or earlobes with obsidian blades and offered the blood to the gods, a bloodletting practice shared with the Maya and understood as opening a channel to the supernatural.

Rulers were depicted wearing jaguar-skin garments and sitting on jaguar thrones, part of a pan-Mesoamerican tradition of jaguar symbolism traceable to the Olmec in which the jaguar represented royal power and the supernatural.

Monte Albán survived Teotihuacan's collapse around 550 CE and endured for another two centuries, its resilience demonstrating that the Zapotec capital's survival did not depend on its diplomatic partner.

title: Monte Albán Falls Silent
bullets: Monte Albán's decline unfolded over roughly 200 years from 600 to 800 CE, driven not by invasion or natural disaster but by the convergence of lost trade networks, agricultural stress, and the growing independence of rival valley centers.

Teotihuacan's collapse around 550 CE severed Monte Albán's most important diplomatic and trade connection, causing exotic goods like central Mexican obsidian and marine shell to become scarcer over subsequent decades.

Thirteen centuries of intensive farming, canal irrigation, and population growth may have degraded the valley's soils, with evidence of erosion on hillsides and expansion of agriculture into more marginal areas.
Lambityeco in the Tlacolula arm gained prominence during the decline, its palaces decorated with remarkable stucco friezes including paired portrait heads of a ruling couple and large Cocijo masks.

Yagul's Palace of the Six Patios was a complex of interconnected courtyards surrounded by rooms, embodying the shared-power arrangements of the post-Monte Albán political order with multiple elite lineages in connected quarters.

By around 750 CE the Main Plaza was no longer maintained, the North Platform was abandoned, no new stelae were carved, and families were methodically stripping usable materials from their terraced homes.
Even after abandonment Monte Albán remained a sacred burial ground, with Zapotec and later Mixtec elites reopening ancient tombs to deposit new offerings alongside centuries-old bones.
The Zapotec civilization survived the loss of its capital: the language, pottery traditions, religion, and calendar all continued unbroken, proving more durable than any single city.

title: The Mixtec Wars and Mitla
bullets: After Monte Albán's decline, Zapotec political power split between secular authority centered at Zaachila, whose rulers claimed Monte Albán descent, and spiritual authority at Mitla, seat of the huijatoo high priest.

Mitla's palaces were decorated with unique geometric stone mosaic panels made from thousands of precisely cut stone pieces fitted without mortar into repeating fret, spiral, and step patterns found nowhere else in the pre-Columbian Americas.

The huijatoo, the Zapotec high priest at Mitla, wielded spiritual authority the Spanish compared to the Pope, presiding over royal burials and guarding what was believed to be the literal entrance to the underworld.

Mixtec populations expanded into the Valley of Oaxaca beginning around 1000 CE through a combination of military conquest, dynastic intermarriage, and migration into areas depopulated after Monte Albán's decline.

The Mixtec warlord 8 Deer Jaguar Claw from 1063 to 1115 CE conquered most of the Mixteca and parts of the Valley of Oaxaca, his life recorded in painted codices with a detail rivaling medieval European chronicles.

In 1932 Alfonso Caso opened Tomb 7 at Monte Albán and found over 500 objects of gold, turquoise, jade, and crystal deposited by Mixtec elites around 1350 CE, the richest archaeological find in Mexican history.

The goldwork from Tomb 7 was made using lost-wax casting, a technique that arrived in Mesoamerica from South America via Pacific coast maritime trade around 800 CE, with the Mixtec becoming Mesoamerica's master goldsmiths.

Around 1400 CE Zapotec rulers extended control to the Isthmus of Tehuantepec, gaining Pacific coast trade access and a geographic buffer, their Istmeño descendants remaining one of the most culturally vital Zapotec communities today.

Caso's decades of systematic excavation at Monte Albán established the Period I through V chronological framework that archaeologists still use, transforming the site from an overgrown ruin into one of the best-documented in the Americas.

title: Conquest and Survival
bullets: In 1458 CE Aztec armies under Motecuhzoma I invaded the Valley of Oaxaca, conquering Mixtec towns and imposing tribute obligations demanding cacao, gold, cochineal dye, cotton, and feathered cloaks.

Cosijoeza, king of Zaachila whose name means lightning commands respect, retreated to the mountain fortress of Guiengola on the Isthmus of Tehuantepec and withstood a prolonged Aztec siege under Emperor Ahuitzotl.

The siege ended with a diplomatic marriage: Cosijoeza married Coyolicatzin, a daughter of the Aztec emperor, preserving Zapotec internal autonomy while acknowledging Aztec tributary authority, a deal few conquered peoples achieved.

Cosijopii, son of Cosijoeza and the Aztec princess, submitted to Spanish forces under Francisco de Orozco in 1521 after the fall of Tenochtitlan, choosing negotiation over the military destruction that had devastated the Aztec capital.
After publicly converting to Christianity Cosijopii was caught practicing Zapotec religious rites in secret, tried for idolatry by the Spanish authorities, and stripped of his political power.

Across colonial Mexico indigenous peoples practiced syncretism, blending pre-conquest gods with Catholic saints and timing old festivals to coincide with Catholic feast days, with the Zapotec maintaining this tradition with particular determination.

Today more than one million people in Oaxaca speak Zapotec languages across at least four major language clusters and dozens of local varieties, a linguistic diversity reflecting centuries of geographic isolation and political fragmentation.

Monte Albán is a UNESCO World Heritage Site visited by hundreds of thousands of tourists annually, its Main Plaza, Danzantes, Building J, and tombs maintained by INAH, with Tomb 7 treasures displayed at the Museo de las Culturas de Oaxaca.

The Genealogical Lienzos, painted cloth documents produced by Zapotec communities in the sixteenth century, served simultaneously as works of art, legal documents defending indigenous land claims, and acts of cultural resistance.
